name: Tom Kha Pladuk (Catfish Soup)
recipeIngredient: 1 cat fish (about half a pound prepared weight)
1 tablespoon prik ki nu daeng (red birdseye chilis), thinly sliced
recipeInstructions:
1. Bring the stock to a simmer.
2. Add the galangal, lemon grass, coriander, chilis, fish sauce, and lime juice, and bring back to the simmer.
3. Clean the fish and cut it into 1-inch steaks, then divide them, removing the bones.
4. Add the fish to the soup, and stir gently to combine with the coconut milk. Bring back to a very gentle simmer.
5. Poach the fish for 3-4 minutes (until just cooked).
6. Serve hot.
NOTES:
There are two staple soups in Thai cuisine: tom yam is a hot spicy clear soup with elements of sweet and sour flavors added. Tom kha is a milder soup with coconut milk and galangal (kha) dominating rather than the fiery chili (prik) of tom yam.
Because it is milder, tom khas are often made with chicken or pork, but most commonly in Thailand are varieties using seafood (especially shrimp, squid, or fish such as red snapper or catfish) or vegetables (especially medleys of mushrooms, tomatoes, and onions).
The catfish can be "crisped" by quickly deep frying it in very hot oil, but this variation is not used here. In Thailand, the fish is cleaned, poached whole with the head, removed from the soup, and cut into bite-sized pieces which are returned to the soup for serving. The method here is simpler, avoiding handling the hot fish.
Thais eat the galangal, which can be left as thin matchstick pieces or crushed with a mallet and then discarded by the diner. Similarly, lemon grass is eaten but may be cut into 2-inch lengths, crushed, and also discarded by the diner.
